title: Lesson: 1.5 Teaching Principles
content:
Lesson Title: 1.5 Teaching Principles
Description: 
Type: blocks
Icon: Video

title: Content: 1.5 Teaching Principles
content:
Heading:

Teaching Principles

Paragraph:

There are several teaching principles that teachers should keep in mind as they teach writing in the early grades. As you review the chart below, note which items you already use while teaching writing and identify at least one item you would like to use more often. You can download a copy of the chart below.

title: Content: 1.5 Teaching Principles
content:
Paragraph:

Gradual Release of Responsibility

This model of instruction is also referred to as an I do it, We do it, You do it approach to teaching. It includes explicit instruction using a direct approach to introduce and teach a new writing skill, strategy, or technique (Pearson & Gallagher, 1983). Explicit instruction includes explaining a skill and modeling how it is applied using think-aloud, and providing guided practice with feedback. 

Systematic  Instruction of Writing Strategies

Systematic instruction refers to a planned and logical sequence of teaching, proceeding in small steps and checking for student understanding before moving to the next step. In the early grades, this includes a scope and sequence that moves from drawing, to labeling and lists, to sentences, to paragraphs. 

Differentiated Instruction

Differentiated instruction calls for designing instruction to suit individual student needs rather than using a standardized approach to instruction that assumes all students learn to write the same way. 

Scaffolding

Scaffolding is assistance offered by a teacher or a peer to support learning a writing skill that a student is initially unable to grasp independently, and then removal of the assistance once the skill is learned. This is not the same as doing the work for the student. Scaffolding is a hallmark of differentiated instruction. 

Use Multi-Sensory Activities

Teach writing skills by including activities that incorporate auditory, visual, tactile, and kinesthetic pathways for learning. 

Automaticity Through Practice

When students learn a writing skill at an automatic (mastery) level, they have learned it so thoroughly that they can use it with little or no conscious attention. In particular, teachers should strive to develop automatic spelling and handwriting skills so students can focus their attention on the composing aspects of writing. Significant guided practice is an essential element of developing automaticity.

title: Content: 1.5 Teaching Principles
content:
Heading:

More About the Gradual Release of Responsibility

Paragraph:

The teacher is the centerpiece of the I do it stage, employing an explicit and systematic approach that includes modeling and think aloud to introduce and teach a writing skill. At the We do it stage, students practice the skill in whole group or small groups. The teacher guides this practice and provides targeted, corrective feedback to students. During this stage, the teacher differentiates instructional support and provides scaffolds based on the individual needs of students. At the You do it stage, students are able to use the skill independently. The amount of guided practice that students need to reach the independent level varies from student to student. Listen to Joan Sedita, author of Keys to Early Writing, explain this model.

title: Content: 1.5 Teaching Principles
content:
Heading: Heading Paragraph:

The steps below gradually release responsibility for learning and using a writing skill, strategy, or technique to students:

title: Content: 1.5 Teaching Principles
content:
Paragraph:

Identify, define, explain the writing skill. Explicitly teach the skill using modeling and think aloud.

Paragraph:

Show the skill in model writing samples. Show students writing samples that incorporate the skill and help them analyze how the author applied the skill.

Paragraph:

Practice skill-building activities. Provide opportunities for students to practice the skill.

Paragraph:

Modify and revise writing samples. Have students practice applying the skill to writing samples that are partially completed or that need “fixing”. The samples can be generated by the teacher, or they can be anonymous student samples. Have students work collaboratively to apply the skill to improve the samples.

Paragraph:

Use the skill in a collaborative writing piece. Provide opportunities for students to work collaboratively to complete a writing task that incorporates the skill. Give feedback about the use of the skill, and find opportunities for students to revise their pieces using this feedback.

Paragraph:

Students use the skill independently. When students are ready, have them independently complete a writing piece that incorporates the writing skill. Continue to provide feedback and opportunities for revision.

title: Content: 1.5 Teaching Principles
content:
Heading:

Mentor Text

title: Content: 1.5 Teaching Principles
content:
Paragraph:

Most people learn to write by emulating others, similar to learning other new skills such as playing a sport or a musical instrument. Writing models, also known as mentor text, are used to demonstrate strong writing for students so they can imitate style, language, and structure in their own writing.  Joan Sedita, author of Keys to Early Writing, explains mentor text in the video below.

title: Content: 1.5 Teaching Principles
content:
Heading: Heading Paragraph:

Mentor models can be used to show how authors use writing strategies and techniques, also called writing moves. Portalupi and Fletcher (2001) explain that students need to “apprentice themselves” to good writers as they learn to write. Mentor text may be used broadly to teach students the difference between opinion, informational, and narrative text. It can also be used more specifically to teach students specific writing techniques, such as how to use shapes to draw, how to write an expanded sentence, how to write a topic sentence for a paragraph, or how to use transition words and phrases such as finally, in addition, another. 

You will learn more about using mentor text in Module 4 Writer's Craft and Text Structure. A list of examples of mentor text that can be used to teach techniques and text structures shared in this course can be found at the end of Module 9 Wrap Up.

title: Content: 1.5 Teaching Principles
content:
Heading:

Knowledge Check

Paragraph:

When we show up to the present moment with all of our senses, we invite the world to fill us with joy. The pains of the past are behind us. The future has yet to unfold. But the now is full of beauty simply waiting for our attention.

title: Content: 1.5 Teaching Principles
content:
Title:

The teacher provides a graphic organizer to help students plan a writing piece and a list of transition words to reference while writing. This is an example of which teaching principle?

title: Content: 1.5 Teaching Principles
content:
Title:

The teacher wants students to write longer, complete sentences and provides three examples of well-written sentences. This is an example of which teaching principle?

title: Content: 1.5 Teaching Principles
content:
Heading:

Supplemental Resources

Paragraph:
  • Video: Gradual Release of Responsibility (Doug Fisher)
  • Video: Explicit Instruction (Anita Archer)
  • Article: Differentiated Instruction for Writing (Reading Rockets)

title: Content: 1.5 Teaching Principles
content:
Heading:

Teaching Principles

Paragraph:

There are several teaching principles that teachers should keep in mind as they teach writing in the early grades. As you review the chart below, note which items you already use while teaching writing and identify at least one item you would like to use more often. You can download a copy of the chart below.

content:
Paragraph:

Gradual Release of Responsibility

title: Content: 1.5 Teaching Principles
content:
Heading:

Mentor Text

content:
Paragraph:

title: Video: 1.5 Teaching Principles - Video Content: I, We, You Edited Captions
content:
Video: I, We, You Edited Captions
Duration: 291.89 seconds
Resolution: 1280x720
FPS: 29.97002997002997
Transcript: Let's revisit the gradual release of responsibility model that that we've talked about before that term is often associated with Pearson and Gallagher I like a kind of simpler version of it the eye we you model or approach to teaching and this is something that is infused in just about every keys to literacy professional development program it's something that we try to model with you as teachers so as trainers we're at the I stage being very explicit with you modeling things we give you a chance to practice it and eventually we want you to independently start to use some of the practices with your students but that's what we want to do for our students as well so as we're teaching them some of the writing skills some of the content writing strategies we want to bring them through this cycle and as we introduce any new skill in this collection of skills O whether it be how you mark your text how you take your twocolumn notes how you transfer the notes into writing we want to follow the best instructional practices represented in gradual relief O the I stage represents it's the teacher the instructor and the highlights for really good eye instruction are modeling and one of the ways we do that is by using think aloud so we literally are talking through what's what we're thinking about as we do each move in a skill that we're using a lot of explicit instruction very systematic so kind of breaking the skill down into its component pieces but very quickly we need to move to the we stage now the we stage is really represents guided practice so this is where now the students start to get to practice this stuff but I want to emphasize the word guided practice because if someone only sees something once and they start trying to use it and they use it wrong they end up developing really bad habits unless they're given some feedback for how to fix those mistakes so the Wii is about practice but it's guided practice an that guidance can come from the teacher but it can also come from the students their peer students guided practice can happen whole class it can happen in small collaborative groups as it relates to writing we know that when kids have opportunities to work with their peers to practice at the think or the plan or the right or the revised stage their writing improves faster but it can also happen let's say for homework but then they're given some feedback afterwards what's our ultimate goal our ultimate goal is independent use by the students OK now when we talk about some big terms in education differentiation you know scaffolding right how does that overlay to this well what's what's we have to remember is that every student goes through these stages at different levels so you may have some really advanced writers some you know high Flyers we call them you know these are the kids that just get everything really with just a little bit of explicitness a little bit of guided practice they're often running with this stuff right but you may have other students that really need to spend a lot more time here most of our students even our struggling ones can get to the independent youth stage right it's just that they need so much more guided practice and support in order to do that and that's where the scaffolds come in if we have got some students that very quickly understand how to take two column notes well we can just say take two calm notes they do it on blank paper if we've got some other students that aren't as on board we might give them a set of partially scaffolded notes where maybe our main ideas are there are some of our details when we get to the part in the keys to content writing routine where we talk about using models and using scaffolds that's where things like giving kids sets of steps or writing templates to complete so that's all the kinds of scaffolding we do to help kids move from the we to the you so I'd like to you to just be keeping this in the back of your mind as you teach any of the elements in this routine it's more an awareness than anything else but it is absolutely crucial if we want to get kids from things away from things being all teachers centered to eventually independent use by students

and one of the ways we do that is by using think aloud so we literally are talking through what's what we're thinking about as we do each move in a skill that we're using a lot of explicit instruction very systematic so kind of breaking the skill down into its component pieces but very quickly we need to move to the we stage now the we stage is really represents guided practice so this is where now the students start to get to practice this stuff but I want to emphasize the word guided practice because if someone only sees something once and they start trying to use it and they use it wrong they end up developing really bad habits unless they're given some feedback for how to fix those mistakes so the Wii is about practice but it's guided practice an that guidance can come from the teacher but it can also come from the students their peer students guided practice can happen whole class it can happen in small collaborative groups as it relates to writing we know that when kids have opportunities to work with their peers to practice at the think or the plan or the right or the revised stage their writing improves faster but it can also happen let's say for homework but then they're given some feedback afterwards what's our ultimate goal our ultimate goal is independent use by the students OK now when we talk about some big terms in education differentiation you know scaffolding right how does that overlay to this well what's what's we have to remember is that every student goes through these stages at different levels so you may have some really advanced writers some you know high Flyers we call them you know these are the kids that just get everything really with just a little bit of explicitness a little bit of guided practice they're often running with this stuff right but you may have other students that really need to spend a lot more time here most of our students even our struggling ones can get to the independent youth stage right it's just that they need so much more guided practice and support in order to do that and that's where the scaffolds come in if we have got some students that very quickly understand how to take two column notes well we can just say take two calm notes they do it on blank paper if we've got some other students that aren't as on board we might give them a set of partially scaffolded notes where maybe our main ideas are there are some of our details when we get to the part in the keys to content writing routine where we talk about using models and using scaffolds that's where things like giving kids sets of steps or writing templates to complete so that's all the kinds of scaffolding we do to help kids move from the we to the you so I'd like to you to just be keeping this in the back of your mind as you teach any of the elements in this routine it's more an awareness than anything else but it is absolutely crucial if we want to get kids from things away from things being all teachers centered to eventually independent use by students
